text: The surgeon carefully unfolds the loop of the sigmoid colon from deep within the abdominal cavity, encountering adhesions likely resulting from a previous hysterectomy and salpingo-oophorectomy. Mesh from a sacral colpopexy is visible extending from the vaginal cuff.
The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection at the sacral promontory, creating a window beneath the superior hemorrhoidal vessel and carefully taking the vessel during the dissection process.

The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection at the sacral promontory, creating a window underneath the superior hemorrhoidal artery while considering the presence of mesh from previous surgeries.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, taking the vessel while avoiding ureteral catheters or stents. The ureter is visible in the surgical field. The surgeon proceeds with a right-sided total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, carefully navigating around the mesh present in the area.

The surgeon performs a right-sided total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, navigating around the mesh visible on the right side of the rectum. The camera is positioned in arm three, providing a limited view of the dissection area due to the mesh.
The surgeon operates with the camera positioned in arm three, providing a limited view of the right side of the rectum due to the presence of mesh. The surgeon maneuvers to navigate around the mesh.
The surgeon observes an uncoated proline mesh within the peritoneal cavity, noting its placement below the right-sided rectal mesentery, which is typically extraperitoneal in similar cases.
The surgeon observes the placement of an uncoated proline mesh within the peritoneal cavity, noting its intraperitoneal position beneath the right-sided rectal mesentery.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection around the rectum, avoiding mobilization of the splenic flexure, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection without mobilizing the splenic flexure, focusing on routine and standard techniques.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of a sponge to protect the ureter during dissection, ensuring safety without the use of ICG. The bowel is observed to have areas of thickened, woody tissue due to significant diverticulosis.

The surgeon demonstrates the use of a sponge to protect the ureter during dissection, allowing for safe tissue manipulation without the risk of injury. The bowel appears thickened and woody due to significant diverticulosis, although the patient has not experienced diverticulitis.

The surgeon prepares to make an anastomosis proximal to the previously dissected area. After completing the lateral dissection, the surgeon begins pelvic dissection, carefully checking the ureter to avoid injury in this critical area.

The surgeon prepares to make an anastomosis proximal to the previously dissected area. After completing the lateral dissection, the surgeon proceeds with the pelvic dissection, carefully checking for the ureter to avoid injury.

The surgeon performs left-sided dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, navigating around a proximal and distal tattoo. The distal tattoo is located just above the peritoneal reflection, approximately three centimeters distal to the tumor. Anterior dissection is carried out in the rectovaginal septum, with the mesh visibly not integrated into the anterior rectum.

The surgeon performs left-sided dissection in the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, navigating around the proximal and distal tattoos. The distal tattoo is located just above the peritoneal reflection, approximately three centimeters distal to the tumor, guiding the dissection below this mark.
The surgeon performs anterior dissection in the rectovaginal septum, revealing that the mesh has not integrated into the anterior rectum.

The surgeon selects the distal margin and uses a vessel sealer to clear the mesentery, ensuring the mesh is out of the way. The robust, thickened omentum is maneuvered into the pelvis, positioned between the bowel, anastomosis, and mesh.
The surgeon selects the distal margin and uses a vessel sealer to clear the mesentery, ensuring the mesh is out of the way. The planned anastomosis is positioned just below the level of the mesh.

The surgeon completes the clearance of the rectal mesentery and proceeds to dissect the proximal mesentery, approaching from beneath the transection of the superior hemorrhoidal artery, while carefully preserving the left colic artery.
The surgeon completes the clearance of the rectal mesentery and proceeds to dissect the proximal mesentery, approaching from beneath the transection of the superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon carefully preserves the left colic artery during the dissection, ensuring adequate blood supply to the colon due to the patient's cardiac morbidity and vasculopathy.
The surgeon prepares to staple the bowel after taking the mesentery, using ICG to ensure clear margins for the intracorporeal anastomosis of the left colon.
The surgeon staples the bowel after taking the mesentery, using ICG fluorescence to ensure clear margins. The intracorporeal anastomosis technique is applied to the left colon.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn two-layer anastomosis at or just below the level of the peritoneal reflection, joining the two posterior staple lines. The camera is repositioned from port three to port two, allowing the surgeon to work with two right hands and one left hand for improved visibility.
The surgeon prepares to perform a hand-sewn two-layer anastomosis, avoiding the use of an EEA stapler and opting for intracorporeal techniques.

The surgeon operates at or just below the level of the peritoneal reflection, using two right hands and one left hand for improved visualization. The camera is positioned at port two, and the surgeon joins the two posterior staple lines.
The surgeon begins the proctotomy, cutting into the rectum. The visual reveals unexpected bowel contents due to the patient's lack of bowel prep, visible in both the rectum and proximal bowel.
The surgeon proceeds to make a proctotomy, revealing the contents of the rectum and proximal bowel due to the patient's lack of bowel preparation.
The surgeon begins the inner row of the hand-sewn anastomosis, starting at the midline and joining the two posterior lips, progressing towards the patient's left side.
The surgeon begins the inner row of the anastomosis, starting at the midline and joining the two posterior lips, progressing towards the patient's left side.

The surgeon parks the suture temporarily and begins suturing from the midline towards the patient's right side, maneuvering around the right corner. The suture is then passed over the top from the patient's right to left.
The surgeon parks the suture temporarily and begins suturing in the midline, moving towards the patient's right side and around the right corner.
The surgeon sutures from the patient's right to left, bringing the left-sided suture towards the midline to avoid meeting at a corner. The common channel is completely closed with the sutures.

The surgeon adjusts the left-sided suture towards the midline to avoid meeting at a corner, ensuring the posterior row remains visible for starting the right-sided inner suture. The suture is then continued from right to left, closing the common channel.
The surgeon ties the two sutures together and uses the original suture to imbricate over the top of the joined posterior staple lines, ensuring a secure closure.
The surgeon ties two sutures together and uses the original suture to imbricate over the top of the joined posterior staple lines, ensuring secure closure.

The surgeon performs a leak test on the bowel anastomosis following a sigmoid resection, ensuring the integrity of the surgical connection. Omentum is placed between the mesh and the bowel anastomosis to provide support and separation.
